MAGIC-SOCIETY
item.description: Individuals. Souls. Long-term sanity. Static traditions. Weak bloodlines. Magic. Controlled territories. Sacrifice reserves. Cinders. Youthful vigor Decentralized, merit-based, brutal. Spell-weavers hold battlefield command. Cinders manage ritual authority and provide prophetic guidance. No eternal rulers. Tenures end in burnout or challenge. Leadership shifts with magical potency. "Better to burn fast than be erased slowly." The Mist devours the timid; fire claims the bold. Cinder magic is the world's true pulse - stasis invites Hollowing. Every flare extends humanity's defiant light. Fatalistic youth-heavy society. Reveres those who burn brightly. Conscription from adolescence; training in pain tolerance. Relationships forged in ritual circles, dissolved by burnout. These states normalize Hollowing. Identity tied to cinder-mark (personal magic scar, acquired or branded on at birth). Marriages as power alliances (merging lines of powerful casters). Trade in crystals, soul-essences, scrolls. Opportunistic raids on Husk clusters for magical reagents. Currency secondary to raw potency (measured in burn-rate) Aggressive flare-strikes, never prolonged defenses. Hit-and-burn: ignite, retreat, repeat. Drag foes into cinder-zones for mutual Hollowing. Weaponize burnout with suicide spells as last resort. Burnout epidemics (mass Hollowing cascades). Youth dependency, little age-granted wisdom. Internal purges (challenges fracture unity). Pyre Coast Ashkara The Cinderbound Vel Ignis Burning Reach Searfall The Red Oathlands Kindlepoint Vathen Flame Emberfold Ashkara Emberfold Searfall Pyraxis Vathen Kindrel Cindros Ignara Fyreholt Velkar Ashen Pyrel Cindra Vahl Kareth Ignis Sarrox Emra Volen Kyr

item.title: NOMAD-SOCIETY
item.description: Territory Settlements Outsiders Material surplus The weak who slow the caravan Vehicles (these are lineage, inheritance, identity) Bloodlines / crews Routes and path-knowledge Mobility itself Decentralized, vehicle-based Authority lies with: Lead drivers (navigation authority) Mechanics (survival authority) Path-readers / Mist-sensitives (mythic authority) No “king.” At best: Convoy leaders (temporary, situational) Leadership changes based on context: Travel = driver leads Breakdown = mechanic leads Danger = path-reader leads Authority shifts mid-scene depending on the problem. “Standing still is death.” “The world belongs to what moves through it.” “We don’t fight the Mist - we outlive it.” “Settlers choose their graves. We don’t.” No permanent homes → everything modular, collapsible, strapped down Children raised on the move, taught roles early Relationships are practical first, emotional second Identity = your vehicle + your crew Marriages = merging crews / resources Exile = being left behind (worse than death) Trade is opportunistic: salvage fuel parts information about routes Currency is secondary to utility They read: wind shifts in the Mist sound distortion Husk movement rhythms Engine rhythms used as signal patterns Horns, chants, or drumbeats to guide or disperse Husks Smoke mixtures that mask or redirect presence Path-readers are: respected feared possibly "other" Dependence on vehicles → breakdown = crisis Limited heavy infrastructure → can’t hold ground Internal fragmentation → clans may not cooperate Resource bottlenecks (fuel, parts, specialists) Optional nasty one: Aging problem → older members become liabilities if they can’t keep up The Ashwheel Convoy Ironwake Drift The Gutterline Clans Brassroad Nomads The Coilbound Procession Sprocket Mile Syndicates The Rust Atlas Caravans Gearwake Circles The Cinder Axle Union Varnish Road Tribes The Long Dust March Red Horizon Lines The Endless Switchback The Passing Mile Kin The Vanishing Route The Driftway Peoples The No-Return Convoy The Betweenroads The Passing Weather Clans The Borrowed Ground Travelers Children of the Open Engine The Unmoored Bloodlines Those Who Refuse Stillness The Last Moving Kin The Oath of No Place The Windborne Remnants The Surviving Mile The Hollowroad Families The Quiet Departure Clans The Ones Who Did Not Settle

item.title: IMAGES
HEROIC-SOCIETY
item.description: Regular troops. Equipment budgets. Failed/Old prototypes. Conscripts' comfort. National pride/symbols. Prestige tech. Heroic exceptions. Propaganda machine. Adaptive doctrine. Split: mass-mobilization commanders for hordes. Heroic exceptions for superweapon ops. Populist high command (voted in on victory promises). Tech-overseers. Authority rotates with results—failures demote fast. "One decisive breakthrough will end this." Every martyr's wave buys time for the superweapon. Heroes prove the horde's unbreakable spirit. Learning from mistakes hones the winning edge. Propaganda-saturated, populist fervour. Hero worship; quick canonization of dead aces. Mass rallies double as recruitment drives. Forgets rank-and-file dead; elevates the exceptional. Identity via banner affiliation or hero cult. Marriages incentivized for breeding cannon fodder. Exile as "banner-stripping"—public shaming. Total war footing: farms feed troops, factories churn prototypes. Trade in scrap from failed mechs, hero relics. Barter over currency; loyalty points for service Overwhelming human waves pin enemies. Superweapons (mechs, beams) for kill shots—glorious even in failure. Diversion tactics: feint with fodder, strike with prototypes. Post-battle reviews adapt for next wave. Gear shortages cripple horde effectiveness. Superweapon unreliability (budget black holes). Morale crashes on repeated prototype flops. The Vanguard Republic Crownsteel The March of Banners Ironblood Pact Brighthammer The Forward Compact Lionsreach The Bannered Host Steelwake The Proving Lands Brighthammer Crownsteel Ironwake Vanguard Bannerfall Steelreach Lionmark Marcher Proving Redhold Valon Bast Rurik Marrek Tarn Solvar Kord Draven Hesk Brann

item.title: DIESEL-SOCIETY
item.description: Empathy. Autonomy. Sometimes truth. Individual machines. Human middle-management. Leadership cadre. Data archives. Continuity (uninterrupted production lines). Diesel reserves (lifeblood). Core directives. Centralized, abstracted, distant. High algorithms/directorate oversee strategy. Factory-overseers manage servant production. Field coordinators for droid legions. Humans insulated; dissent rerouted to simulations. "War can be solved, eventually." Machines endure so people thrive. Diesel autonomy frees humanity from chains. Data proves iterative victory inevitable. Insulated comfort; servants handle labour/war. Technocratic surveillance ensures efficiency. Risk-averse elite; failures blamed on "glitches." Identity via directive affiliation (e.g., Sector-7 Citizen). Pairings optimized by compatibility algorithms. Exile as "decommissioning" - reassignment to fringes. Diesel/steel barter; factories as currency hubs. Surplus servants traded for rare fuels. Self-sustaining loops minimize human input. Swarm droid waves overwhelm foes. Heavy diesel constructs (tanks, walkers) for breakthroughs. A specialised unit for any situation. Autonomous adaptations mid-battle via uplinks. Civilian safety paramount evac them first. Diesel dependency - supply lines. Centralization bottleneck. Servant glitches from overuse. Elite detachment breeds resentment. The Directorate of Axiom Cold Meridian Heliox The Calculant States Northvector Ordinance The Glass Authority Prime Relay Echelon Reach Vault Union Heliox Axiom Northvector Meridian Ordinance Relay Echelon Calibre Vaultis Sigil Astrax Helion Virex Omnis Nulla Cyr Axion Quant Nex Orbis

item.title: CLASSICAL-SOCIETY
item.description: Time. Frontline soldiers. Resources in meatgrinders. Souls - the ones sent into battle and between folders and forms alike. Cities. Command continuity. Industry. Borders. National morale. Rigid hierarchy, layered bureaucracy. General staff for grand strategy. Trench officers enforce doctrine. Logistics commissars ration steel. Political overseers tie service to citizenship. "Stability through endurance." Lines held today secure tomorrow's world. Sacrifice carves victory from time. Balance tempers fire and machine. Conservative, rule-heavy; service grants citizenship. Glorifies patience, sacrifice via memorials. Slow-moving bureaucracy insulates homefront. Identity via regiment or home trench. Marriages arranged for stable lineages. Court-martials and executions for insubordination, both in the trench and on the streets. Rationed output: shells, food, conscripts. Trade alliances for raw ore. War bonds fund the grind. Trench sentries map Husk patrols via flares. Artillery barrages on swarm patterns. HOLD THE LINE: bunkers, wire, massed guns. Artillery softens, infantry anchors. Countercharges reclaim lost earth. Rotations prevent collapse. Stagnation (doctrinal rigidity). Attrition burnout (manpower drain). Supply vulnerabilities. Karsfeld The Bastioned Marches Vireholt The Grey Compact Dornreach Hearthline Union Stonewake The Lowwall Authority Graven Front Iron Vale Karsfeld Dornreach Viremark Stonewake Graven Lowmarch Hearthline Ironvale Brask Coldholt Karsin Dreva Volmark Hadrum Belroth Seln Mireth Ordan Caldrin Vorsk

item.title: EROSION
item.description:
Magic doesn’t want to be inside you. Veins, fingernails, and even eyes may begin glowing in red/yellow hues. That's the usual sign of magic accumulation, which, if left untreated, can cause "burnups" "Treating it" means releasing the magic outward, which itself has a chance for burning the person, but also their surroundings if uncontrolled) Usually the Hearth's bond is enough to allow for venting as well as controlled releases of build up magic, but ice, water, and other means of staying cool are what the general public has to work with. The "end product" of the magic accumulation's prolonged effect on a human body is being turned into a Husk - zombie-like shell of a being that resembles a burn victim at least, and becomes vaguely human at worst. A Husk isn’t undead in the usual sense. Collapse = total soul overload. What remains is: A body whose tissues have been thermally vitrified from the inside out. A mind erased by the intensity of the experience. A shell still moving because the residual heat hasn’t dissipated yet. Moral Baseline Shift When everyone knows: Collapse is possible Husks exist And they were people yesterday The moral bar drops. “I’m a liar. I’m a cheat. But I haven’t collapsed.” That’s enough. Bravery gets you: Overexertion Battlefield surges Collapse Cowards: Flee early Hoard less power Survive. There is no redemption in collapse. No lesson. No chosen fate. A Hollow isn’t punished, sinful, or unworthy. They’re what happens when the system fails.


Just as Dangerous (For Different Reasons) Uncontrolled release disturbs the local atmosphere. Side effects: Fire behaving wrong (screaming flames, fire that resembles faces) Attracting Hollows Creating permanent hot zones

WET RATS
item.description: Black Market Releases, the best alternative to the CRS Because of course. Illegal venting in slums. Portable release charms. Collapse-for-hire sabotage. Most Hollow outbreaks trace back here. But the alternative, for the unfortunate, is so much worse.

item.title: ab1db9bafd90ccd2293b55c884fcf7fc
WAR
item.description: Is what the peoples of this world call the event that first ignited the spiral of suffering the world's been cast into ever since continues still, but fractured and localised as independent cities and states lunged at the resources near them with little regard for their previous neighbours.New maps are drawn on the regular to picture the supposed control each region holds, however nobody but the Hollows truly rules within the GREY MIST.

item.title: 365e8e0361d99b29bf9a2df80a8cf475
0a7e94bb0078cdadd37bf85247026720
c6cc9dfcb0a8e0c47df1264faa805e45
bfaff1f8064b0c9ca5766ccf38b3337d
THE GREAT HEARTH
item.description: A movement striving to unite all of humanity in one giant Hearth, they believe that will stabilise everyone and bring about the harmony observed among the more stable Hearths. Their methods are faulty, unorthodox, and oftentimes forceful, which turned the public opinion against them after some disastrous attempts at joining 20 people on a city square that no longer exists...Individual identity is the problem. They talk about equilibrium and "global balance". Forced soul-binding rituals Artifact-driven mass merges City-scale bonding attempts Kidnapping Embers first (they’re “keystones”) Because every failure: Produces survivors who swear it almost worked. Creates permanent hot zones they interpret as “holy ground”. Generates Cinders they call Heralds.

item.title: a4cf25dd5d8dcc6fc0f9110981575967 1
THE EARTHBOUND
item.description: These zealots believe in extremist pacifism, claiming that every life spared and death delayed brings HELL closer to catching up, and ridding the world of magic and GREY MIST. They believe that emotions, memories, and identity are the "fuel" that keeps the furnace overtaxed. Their faith is a rigorous practice of ego-death and emotional suppression. By becoming cold inside while alive, they hope to pass through Hell like a ghost through a flame—unprocessed and unnoticed. They take vows of silence and use lead-lined masks to "keep their heat in."

item.title: HEARTHS AND EMBERS
item.description:
People with spiked Prism aptitudes. The cost of power is life. Embers suffer a shortened lifespan, burning their years away the moment this flame blooms within them for the first time. Despite that, it's a gift, one that manifests randomly and powerfully, and comes at a horrid price. Players are called Embers, groups of bonded Embers are called Hearths. This bond acts as a "Resonance Buffer," preventing the individuals from "detonating" or collapsing into a Hollow state. Hearths can form due to: Rituals done by the Embers themselves, or even without the Embers' choice/knowledge via grimoires or other artifacts. Life experiences: a Bond is likely to form during a shared traumatic event, or, for the lucky ones, it can build up after years of closeness with one another. Bonding with other Embers allows for cleaner channelling - which translates nicely into the mechanics of extra energy per party member. Because Embers forget their pasts (due to LORE/WORLD/CULTURAL PILLAR/RELIGION-PHILOSOPHY/EROSION), they often lack a traditional moral compass. They act on instinct, immediate survival, or the orders of whoever is currently guiding them.
Hearth-members (Embers) are known to have blurry memories and little recollection of who they were before the "merge". The bond between them can also be strengthened or weakened by individual interactions, even to the point of breaking (which is a traumatic experience on its own) It is rare to survive a bond breaking, but those who do are called CINDERS, and often lose their grip on reality, turning away from society altogether Everyone in a hearth is a bit of an anchor to everyone else. Usually the other members are enough to "ground" whoever's having a tough time, but in times of high stress the emotions may get amplified/cascadeHearths: Distributed Containment A Hearth is a living heat-sharing network. Soul-binding isn’t mystical romance. It’s engineering under duress; Heat disperses across multiple vessels Panic dampens if others are stable Emotional regulation is forced by proximity Individual spikes become manageable averages 3–5 was not chosen by accident: 2 is unstable (feedback loop) 6+ increases coordination failure 3–5 balances redundancy and control Someone absolutely did the math. On corpses.

item.title: 5bb236043f8a951c43f45ff03382a38c
CINDERS
item.description: Just as there are the Hearth-linked Embers, there are also "stray Embers", or "Cinders". Embers without a Hearth, either because of tragedy, or neglect, they suffer alone. These individuals make for great adversaries. Ranging from "Mad geniuses" who "made it work", to individuals broken by trauma, lashing out at the unjust world.They consume others. A Cinder doesn't have their Hearth; they seek Fuel. They kidnap people and "burn" through them to stabilize themselves. This gives the players a clear, gritty objective: stop the Cinder from using the local village as a life support. Cinder Status: If Cinders "lose their grip on reality," perhaps they are actually still "perceiving" the bond. They might be trying to talk to the ghosts of their former Hearth-mates, or their brains are permanently wired to receive signals that are no longer being sent.Power isn’t skill. It’s lack of restraint mechanisms. No bond damping No long-term planning They burn hot, And they know it. Which makes them terrifying.

FAITHS
item.description:
Deities, or deity-like entities are believed to exist, but there's scant worthwhile evidence besides faith and the burning need for a truth to cling onto. As such many beliefs have emerged after the Great War, and the GREY MIST came...
Many peoples believe that CINDERS themselves are worthy of worship, even going as far as to sacrifice themselves for these "Prophets" and "Immortals" as they call the rogue Embers.

Mentioned separately in the THE GREAT HEARTH note, these cultists sometimes refer to a being that's unnervingly consistent between reports. They speak of a being with three eyes, and a heart floating between its horns, beating in a steady, calm rhythm, as small streaks of blood paint the air around it in a circular pattern. This bloody halo is said to be the inspiration for the cult's doctrine and end goal...
A belief shared mostly by battlefield veterans, who swear the mist can literally devour people and things, insisting its not just as a metaphor for "losing sight of something"...

item.title: HELL
item.description: Hell is not a kingdom of punishment, and it is not a moral scoreboard. It is a processing furnace: a vast metaphysical underworld machine designed to strip souls down, burn off residue, and send what remains into the Hall of Echoes. It was built for throughput, not mercy.Then the Great War happened, and death arrived in quantities the system was never meant to handle. Hell did not break cleanly. It overloaded, backed up, and began to hold what it could not finish processing, decade after decade of spiritual backlog turning the underworld into a clogged pressure chamber. The result is not a dramatic apocalypse but a sustained failure state: a world sitting under an exhausted infernal engine that is still running, still failing, and still trying to catch up.That overflow is the setting’s drive. The pressure leak from Hell becomes the living world’s magic, seeping upward through people, places, and mistakes. Power is therefore not a blessing so much as a symptom: every Ember is living close to the threshold of overload, and every use of magic is a small negotiation with a system that already has too much inside it. This is why fire is easy to start, why control is rare, and why collapse is never far away.Hell also matters as a political and cultural fact, not just a cosmological one. People may not understand the machinery, but they understand the consequences: burnups, Hollowing, the need for venting, the fear of crowds, and the fact that someone who fails to release their pressure safely may become a danger to everyone nearby. That makes the whole world feel like a place built around emergency management, local damage control, and the quiet terror of systems that are always one breakdown away from cascading failure.

item.title: CITIES
PASSIVES
item.description:
Dealing unblocked HP damage grants +1 CHARGE Charge.
Discard the first negative STATUS drawn (by You) each turn.
Start combats with GUILE BRACE/MOMENTUM.
Killing an enemy lets you DRAW 1 Card OR grants 1 ENERGY.

item.title: OWNER_PRIORITY
item.description: To prevent Alpha-gaming, the player who owns the card currently being played has Absolute Veto. They decide the targets, the timing, or whether to discard it for CHARGE charges. Negotiation and cooperation is encouraged, but the Owner's word is law.

item.title: UNIQUE
item.description: Unique Cards are ones gained through Backstory, or Campaign Events, usually crafted by the GM, or in collaboration with the player.

item.title: CURSES
item.description: Adding a CURSES Keyword to a card makes it add a random STATUScard into the shared deck when played, But, it reduces the card's overall cost by 2 ENERGY (effectively -1 reduction as CURSESare still technically keywords and so they increase a card's cost by 1).

item.title: HORDE-SOCIETY
item.description: Expendable: regular troops Preserved: national pride, symbols, prestige tech Authority: split — mass command + heroic exception Justification: “One decisive breakthrough will end this”Politics: Populist Propaganda-heavy Obsessive about image Culture: Loves heroes Loves martyrs Forgets the dead quickly Prototype mechs failing is not a bug here — it’s the point. The Vanguard Republic Crownsteel The March of Banners Ironblood Pact Brighthammer The Forward Compact Lionsreach The Bannered Host Steelwake The Proving Lands Brighthammer Crownsteel Ironwake Vanguard Bannerfall Steelreach Lionmark Marcher Proving Redhold Valon Bast Rurik Marrek Tarn Solvar Kord Draven Hesk Brann These feel personal — good for cultures that mythologise soldiers.

item.title: GM_Guide
item.description: People? Souls? Land? Time? Truth? This is the most important question. Infrastructure? Culture? Leadership? Knowledge? The dead? This determines how they fight. Central command? Local officers? Autonomous units? Ritual specialists? Machines? This shapes governance and combat style. Defensive necessity Religious mandate Historical grievance Economic survival “It’s already too late to stop” This informs propaganda, education, and civilian life.Because war never ended, military logic leaks into civilian life.Examples: Trench states have ration cards, curfews, and long-term planning ministries Ember states have youth conscription, fast promotions, and normalized death Technocratic states have civilian oversight that means nothing This makes politics legible to players.They can feel where they are.

item.title: BURST
item.description:
Each player has an individual Ember Gauge. Charging: Play: +1 Charge per 1 ENERGY spent on your cards.
Sacrifice: +2 Charges per 1 ENERGY cost of a card willingly discarded from hand. You still must pay the card's cost to discard it, but it has no effect unless described otherwise. Charges can stack up indefinitely. Activation:
Costs 10 Charges. Every subsequent activation during one EXPLORATION costs +5 Charges for that particular Ember. After Bursting, the Hearth draws 1 Card less next turn. Subsequent Bursts (even from the same Character) in the same turn, add a stacking +1 to this penalty. (*So a second burst makes the penalty 1 + 1+1. Then the third increases it to 1 + 1+1 + 1+1+1, etc.) Additional Effects: Every 10 Charges stored, gain +1 to the values of ALL your scalable Cards. However, every time you increase this bonus, roll 1d100. Rolling a number lower than your current Charges causes you to take 1d6 DMG for every 10 Charges held.

item.title: BURSTS
item.description:
Sacrifice up to MIGHT VITALS, gain as much BRACE, and 2 X MOMENTUM
Gain GUARD = MIGHT X N (Player Count)
Strike with MOMENTUM multiplier (DMG = Strike X MOMENTUM, instead of Strike + MOMENTUM).
Mend Half Max VITALS, grant MIGHT Burst charges to another Ember.

item.title: BURSTS
item.description: Reduce the cost of all cards currently dealt to 1 this turn. Next card played this turn takes effect 2 extra times (so 3 activations total). Stun an enemy. (Pause their Intent) Draw GUILE cards, regain 1 ENERGY, EXHAUST all STATUS cards currently dealt.

item.title: PASSIVES
item.description:
Leftover GUARD is halved at turn end, instead of vanishing completely.
Your IGNITE effects deal damage at the start of the enemy's turn.
Allies can use Your INSIGHT if its higher than theirs, when playing an INSIGHT-scaling card, once per turn.
EMPOWER INSIGHT the first card played each turn.

item.title: BURSTS
item.description:
Enemy loses all GUARD and COUNTER stacks.
STRIKE LIGHTNING BLEED
Play up to INSIGHT cards for free, if they scale with INSIGHT (from other players too).
IGNITE 2 AREA. Already IGNITEd enemies take their total IGNITE stacks as DMG immediately (Does not remove stacks).

item.title: UNIQUE
item.description: Unique Cards are ones gained through Backstory, or Campaign Events, usually crafted by the GM, or in collaboration with the player.

item.title: CUSTOMS
item.description:
Custom Cards have more than One Keyword. Can be UNIQUE or upgraded from BACKBONE cards.
Card ENERGY Cost Scales with Keyword count (1:1), making complex cards more expensive to play.
They use only the related Prism's Value for effects, where applicable, without the BACKBONE Reliability Bonus.

item.title: BACKBONE
item.description: Starter Cards with only One Keyword, they have an innate +3 Value. Backbone cards are limited to the single-keyword cards provided in the Starting Oath Deck. Adding any keyword or modifying them turns them into Custom cards, removing the +3 bonus. Likewise, removing extra Keywords from a CUSTOMS card brings it back to a Backbone once its left with just One Keyword. Deck Rule: A player’s deck must always contain at least 4 Backbone Cards.

item.title: INJURY
item.description: Cost: 2 Energy. RETAINEXHAUST. While in hand, your BURST is UNPLAYABLE.

item.title: HOLLOW
item.description: Cost: 1 Energy. No effect. Persists between encounters, but dissipates during a LEVEL_UP.

item.title: STATUS
item.description:
STATUS cards are Negative effects applied by Enemies, Events, or through losing VITALS. Unless noted otherwise (by having their ENERGY cost listed), these are UNPLAYABLE, and disappear at the end of combat.
STATUS cards are ownerless, and so are dealt out randomly from left to right to players with the least amount of STATUS cards in hand.

item.title: EXHAUST
item.description:
Cards with this keyword are removed from the deck after being played. They can be restored at a NODE - REST at the cost of 1 SHARDS. Since they remove a card from play, they count against the Minimum Deck limit, as if being removed for the counting.
Reminder: Cannot have less than 4 BACKBONE cards in your deck, meaning that 6/10 (for example) cards in your deck can be Exhaustible, if you wish so.

item.title: PIERCE
item.description: Ignores Block.

item.title: MERCILESS
item.description: If target has no Block, add 2xValue to the card's total Value. Scales with Your highest Prism.

item.title: LIGHTNING
item.description: Hits two targets: One with the lowest and one with the highest HP. In case of only one target available hits it twice.

item.title: AREA
item.description: Affects ALL enemies.

item.title: MIST
item.description: When drawn, shuffle a MIST into the Discard Pile. RETAIN UNPLAYABLE

item.title: TREMBLING
item.description: Cost: 2 Energy. EXHAUST. While in hand, playing another card deals Self DMG = ENERGY cost of the card played.

item.title: Warden

BLEED (5)KILL CIt'll be a hydraesque enemy, that shuffles each head's intent to the right, and, when a head is killed, it loses both its unique intent (ex. Guard), AND a whole column And I know what you're thinking - once they sniff this out they'll kill the Strike and Bleed "heads" and stall the fight And here's the genius of Trembling. Trembling makes you take DMG per Energy spent so the enemy can still damage you without any actual damage intents, BECAUSE YOU STILL NEED TO KILL IT SOMEHOW And if they focus the dmg heads they will allow significant amounts of both trembling and Mist to pollute the deck (mist also self replicates, so it may happen that it will just be ALL you draw at some point)

item.title: Husk
item.description:

GUARD (6)
STRIKE (8)
GUARD (12)HP = 7
Classy 1-2 step with incremental value increases for boyh STRIKE and GUARD. If attacked when showing its first GUARD, switch to first STRIKE instead

item.title: Witch

ADD 2x ASH

item.title: GUARD
item.description: Grants Value Block - stopping incoming DMG. Scales with GUILE

item.title: MEND
item.description:
Restores Value Vitality. Can remove BLEED stacks before they deal damage.
Scales with INSIGHT

item.title: EMBER SHEET
HEARTH SHEET
PANIC
item.description: Cost: 1 Energy. When drawn, DISCARD another card from your hand. It does not contribute to Burst Gauge when discarded this way. RETAIN

item.title: INFECTION
item.description:
UNPLAYABLE. While in hand all Vitals Restoring effects (MEND, VAMPIRIC) deal DMG instead.
*(This does mean you can damage enemies using your MEND cards, and take unavoidable DMG from your VAMPIRIC cards)

item.title: VAMPIRIC
item.description: Restore VITALS equal to 1/2 Value when hitting HP. Stacks.

item.title: BLEED
item.description:
Deal Value DMG at the end of enemy's turn, bypassing GUARD. Can be prevented by Healing (even at max HP/Vitals.) The would-be-healed amount is removed from current BLEED stacks before restoring HP/VITALS
Scales with MIGHT

item.title: IGNITE
item.description:
Deal 1 DMG at the end of the enemy's turn, then decrease stacks by 1. If Stacks reach 9, remove all stacks and deal 9 DMG immediately. Players may choose to Vent their Ignite stacks, taking DMG = no. Stacks cleared. This counts as DMG, interacting with VITALS and MOMENTUM/BRACE rules.
Scales with INSIGHT

item.title: MOMENTUM
item.description:
Pool of Value. When dealing DMG with STRIKE you may expend any amount of stacks from the pool and add it to the STRIKE dealt. Pool decreases when HP damage is taken.
Depending on the SYNC-AXIS; Harmony - halve, rounding up. Discord - remove all stacks.
Scales with MIGHT
Momentum damage is never duplicated, multiplied, or applied to more than one enemy. In the case or AREA being present on the card triggering MOMENTUM, pick a single target to receive the extra damage. In the case of LIGHTNING, the Higher HP target takes the bonus.

item.title: INVENTORY
item.description:
Inventory is strictly divided to separate narrative utility from mechanical progression. Tools (~3 Slots): Functional items (Rope, Flint/Steel, Chalk, Medicine). These grant Advantage (+1 Success) on specific BS ROLLS (CHECKS). Gatekeeping: Certain tasks (climbing a sheer cliff) are impossible (DC ) without the specific item (Rope). Curios (~3 Slots): Personal narrative items or unique trophies. These usually have zero mechanical weight and exist for character flavour or narrative purposes. Share Slots with Tools. Shards (Numerical Value): Weightless essence stored directly in the Ember’s resonance. Coin Pouch: Traditional currency used for mundane trade (food, lodging, information).
Armaments (Swords, Staves, Plate) are Narrative Justifications. They do not occupy slots because they are the physical justification of your 10-card deck. A player doesn't "get a sword" for +1 DMG; they have Strike cards because they have a sword.
These are rare, non-consumable items that sit on the Hearth Sheet (Alongside VITALS, ENERGY, etc.).
They provide non-Oath CHARACTER/OATHS/CONDUIT/PASSIVES to the Hearth, but have either low number values, or specific conditions to make them click. Examples would Include: The Hearth is immune to the first Yellow (Disruptive) Intent of every combat.
When the Sync-Axis is in Harmony (+3 SYNC-AXIS), Mend effects are doubled.
Adds Pierce to the first Strike each turn.
The Hearth can carry 3 Artifacts (Active Passives) Shards do not count toward any inventory limits limit; they are stored within the "Ember Deck" itself. Torch Lantern Rope Lockpicks Crowbar Hammer & Spikes Rations Waterskin Chalk Compass Map Medical Kit Repair Kit Face Wrap A Curio’s effect should be noticeable once every 2–3 fights, and never decisive. Curios can: modify a card tweak a keyword bend a rule slightly Curios should: compete with each other overwrite conflict subtly Should they be optimal? Absolutely not
Grants One of your STRIKE cards VAMPIRIC. If the card is ever removed, the effect is lost with it.
Grants One of your GUARD cards +1 to its Value. Gives +Dice on one check ever (fizzles out afterwards)
Counts as Two SHARDS (can be spent) If you don't draw any of your cards, you can draw 1 more card once per encounter.

item.title: DOUBT
item.description: RETAIN At the end of turn, lose 1 BURSTcharge. UNPLAYABLE

item.title: WOUND
item.description: When drawn, take 2 PIERCE DMG. UNPLAYABLE

item.title: REFERENCE IMAGE
INSIGHT
item.description: Tactical manipulation, force multiplication, and metaphysical stability. Keywords: EMPOWER, WARD, IGNITE, Mend.

item.title: MIGHT
item.description: Physical force, momentum, and brute-force attrition. Keywords: Strike, MOMENTUM, Merciless

item.title: GUILE
item.description: Precision, resource management, and reactive defense. Keywords: Guard, BRACE, Counter, BLEED

item.title: PASSIVES
item.description:
Regain Might VITALS at combat end.
If a STRIKE kills an enemy, all excess damage is applied to a random target.
Discarding for Burst charges grants+3 instead of +2 per ENERGY.
Whenever the Hearth takes VITALS damage, gain +1 Burst Charge.

item.title: DISCARD
item.description: Discard 1 card(s). Cards discarded this way give BURST charges equal to their cost (as if they were played), to their Owner.

item.title: UNPLAYABLE
item.description: Cards with this keyword cannot be played, only Discarded or Exhausted by the effects of other cards, BURST, or other means.

item.title: WARD
item.description: Negates the next negative STATUS or Keyword (except STRIKE) applied by an enemy. Scales with INSIGHT.

item.title: DRAW
item.description: Draw 1 card(s).

item.title: BRACE
item.description:
Pool of Value. When gaining GUARD you may expend any amount of stacks from the pool and add it to the total GUARD received. Pool decreases when HP damage is taken.
Depending on the SYNC-AXIS; Harmony - halve, rounding up. Discord - remove all stacks.
Scales with GUILE

item.title: EMPOWER
item.description: Adds Value to the numeric effect of the next card played. Scales with INSIGHT

item.title: BURSTS
item.description:
Double current MOMENTUM/BRACE stacks.
Gain 2 X Might X GUARD.
Strike with MOMENTUM multiplier (DMG = Strike X MOMENTUM, instead of Strike + 1).
Mend Half Max HP, grant Might Burst charges to another Ember.

item.title: IMAGES
CULTURE
item.description: Nobody riots over curfews. They riot over lantern outages. Common Saying “Don’t be a spark.” That’s an insult. Heroism gets you killed. Core Cultural Virtues Self-control Cowardice (rebranded as prudence) Predictability Collective risk minimization Wealth Is Distance From Heat Rich people: Live in well-lit districts Far from release sites Surrounded by stone, water, and warding Poor people: Live in the Mist Near old burn zones Serve as accidental heat sinks “Keep the lights on. Share the heat. Never trust a spark.”

item.description: REDACTED

item.title: CULTURE
item.description: Nobody riots over curfews. They riot over lantern outages. Common Saying “Don’t be a spark.” That’s an insult. Heroism gets you killed. Core Cultural Virtues Self-control Cowardice (rebranded as prudence) Predictability Collective risk minimization Wealth Is Distance From Heat Rich people: Live in well-lit districts Far from release sites Surrounded by stone, water, and warding Poor people: Live in the Mist Near old burn zones Serve as accidental heat sinks “Keep the lights on. Share the heat. Never trust a spark.”

item.title: CINDERS
item.description: Just as there are the Hearthbonded Embers, there are also "stray Embers", or "Cinders". Embers without a Hearth, either because of tragedy, or neglect, they suffer alone. These individuals are a lore thing mostly, meaning - unplayable, and make for great adversaries. Ranging from "Mad geniuses" who "made it work", to individuals broken by trauma, lashing out at the unjust world. If bonding is so necessary, how do solo adversaries survive? They consume others. A Cinder doesn't have their Hearth; they seek Fuel. They kidnap low-aptitude people and "burn" through them to stabilize their own LORE/WORLD/RELIGION-PHILOSOPHY/EROSION. This gives the players a clear, gritty objective: stop the Cinder from using the local village as a life support. Cinder Status: If Cinders "lose their grip on reality," perhaps they are actually still "perceiving" the bond. They might be trying to talk to the ghosts of their former Hearth-mates, or their brains are permanently wired to receive signals that are no longer being sent. 7. Why They’re Exceptionally Powerful Power isn’t skill. It’s lack of restraint mechanisms. No bond damping No social checks No institutional oversight No long-term planning They burn hot, fast, and wrong. And they know it. Which makes them terrifyingly honest.

item.title: HEARTHS AND EMBERS
item.description:
People with spiked Prism aptitudes. The cost of power is life. Embers suffer a shortened lifespan, burning their years away the moment this flame blooms within them for the first time. Despite that, it's a gift, one that manifests randomly and powerfully, and comes at a horrid price. Players are called Embers, groups of bonded Embers are called Hearths. This bond acts as a "Resonance Buffer," preventing the individuals from "detonating" or collapsing into a Hollow state. Hearths can form due to: Rituals done by the Embers themselves, or even without the Embers' choice/knowledge via grimoires or other artifacts. Life experiences: a Bond is likely to form during a shared traumatic event, or, for the lucky ones, it can build up after years of closeness with one another. Bonding with other Embers allows for cleaner channelling - which translates nicely into extra energy per party member. Because Embers forget their pasts (due to LORE/WORLD/RELIGION-PHILOSOPHY/EROSION), they often lack a traditional moral compass. They act on instinct, immediate survival, or the orders of whoever is currently guiding them. Partially. Hearth-members (Embers) are known to have blurry memories and little recollection of who they were before the "merge". The bond between them can also be strengthened or weakened by individual interactions, even to the point of breaking (which is a traumatic experience on its own) Everyone in a hearth is a bit of an anchor to everyone else. Usually the other members are enough to "ground" whoever's having a tough time, but in times of high stress the emotions may get amplified/cascaded
Yes! It is rare to survive a bond breaking, but those who do are called Cinders, and often lose their grip on reality, turning away from society altogether Probably the second one. I'd imagine them being like social programs, which quickly turn into military ones The most impressive and threatening Embers are those who can output the most, at the smallest cost to their vitals The "Ship of Theseus" Dilemma: If a Hearth replaces a member who died, does the new member eventually "absorb" the residual memories of the previous occupant? This could lead to haunting internal monologues where an Ember cannot distinguish their own childhood from that of a dead stranger. Cascading Failure: If emotions are amplified/cascaded during stress, a single member’s panic attack could theoretically "overclock" the entire Hearth, leading to a collective burnup. This makes emotional discipline as vital as magical talent. Hearths: Distributed Containment (And Why 3–5 Is the Sweet Spot) A Hearth is a living heat-sharing network. Soul-binding isn’t mystical romance. It’s engineering under duress. Why It Works (When It Does) Heat disperses across multiple vessels Panic dampens if others are stable Emotional regulation is forced by proximity Individual spikes become manageable averages You didn’t choose 3–5 by accident. That’s perfect: 2 is unstable (feedback loop) 6+ increases coordination failure 3–5 balances redundancy and control Someone absolutely did the math. On corpses.

item.title: EROSION
item.description: Magic doesn’t want to be inside you. 5. Actually veins, fingernails, and even eyes may begin glowing in red/yellow hues. That's the usual sign of magic accumulation, which, if left untreated, can cause burnups ("treating it" means releasing the magic outward, which itself has a chance for burning the person, but also their surroundings if uncontrolled) 6. Usually the Hearth's bond is enough to allow for venting as well as controlled releases of build up magic, but ice, water, and other means of staying cool are also in play 7. Well technically the "end product" of the magic's effect on a human body is being turned into a Hollow - a husk-like zombie-like shell of a being 8. A Hollow isn’t undead in the usual sense. That implies animation, will, or purpose. This is worse. Magical collapse = total loss of soul containment. What remains is: A body whose tissues have been thermally vitrified from the inside out. A mind erased by overload. A shell still moving because the residual soul-heat hasn’t dissipated yet. Think: walking burnout, not necromancy. 9. The Collapse Timeline Stage 1: Overheat (The Burnups) Chronic fever, ash coughing Skin cracking like fired clay Emotional flattening (empathy burns off early) Fire responds too easily People at this stage know what’s coming. This is where the quote hits: “I could be so much worse.” They still can choose restraint. Stage 2: Breach One moment too much: A stored surge A panicked release A battlefield spike The soul-boundary fails. This is not an explosion. Explosions release energy outward. This is an implosion that's powerful enough to hit the outside too. Memory, identity, intent — gone, into the GREY MIST. Stage 3: Hollowing Organs char but don’t fail completely Blood thickens into slag Nerves fire randomly from thermal stress The heart becomes a smouldering knot The person is already dead. The body just hasn’t realized it yet. Stage 4: The Hollow Walks A Hollow: Avoids nothing Attacks nothing deliberately Moves toward thermal gradients — crowds, fires, battlefields Why? Because residual soul-heat seeks other heat to equalize with. Hollows drift toward the living. That’s why they’re exterminated on sight. Moral Baseline Shift When everyone knows: Collapse is possible Hollows exist And they were people yesterday The moral bar drops. “I’m a liar. I’m a cheat. But I haven’t collapsed.” That’s enough. Bravery gets you: Overexertion Battlefield surges Collapse Cowards: Flee early Hoard less power Survive. There is no redemption in collapse. No lesson. No chosen fate. A Hollow isn’t punished, sinful, or unworthy. They’re what happens when the system fails. Just as Dangerous (For Different Reasons) Uncontrolled release doesn’t just burn the caster — it disturbs the local atmosphere. Side effects: Fire behaving wrong (screaming flames, fire that resembles faces) Attracting Hollows Creating permanent hot zones

item.title: CRS
item.description: Government-independent bodies that handle the population's EROSION. They set up facilities of cold stone and water to allow for safe venting, as well as taking in the "terminal" cases, which they handle out back. They wear layered uniforms, like toxic hazard workers. High-status, low-life-expectancy, trained to read early collapse signs. People accept: Lantern taxes Data indexing Witchhunt-like inspections Not because they trust authority, but because they’ve seen what happens when someone collapses in a crowd.

item.title: WET RATS
item.description: Black Market Releases, the best alternative to the CRS Because of course. Illegal venting in slums. Portable release charms. Collapse-for-hire sabotage. Most Hollow outbreaks trace back here. But the alternative, for the unfortunate, is so much worse.

item.title: GREY MIST
item.description: While EROSION comes from the unprocessed, wandering Souls, the Mist is the Will. It doesn't carry emotions, its suffocatingly cold, and imbued with memories, beliefs. Venturing into it is almost guaranteed to cause hallucinations, temporary personality disorders, and psychosis from mind-homogenisation. The leak is global. Gravity, wind, and geography don’t matter much when the source is below reality. Low light allows it to accumulate. Heat and light disperse it. Hence: Cities burn lanterns at noon Roads are lined with braziers Darkness isn’t romantic — it’s hazardous

item.title: HELL
item.description: Why Hell Is Full: Hell isn’t full because too many people sinned - That’s propaganda. Hell is full because it was designed as a furnace, a bottleneck for the deceased, not the final destination. Souls entered Hell to be broken down, identity stripped, meaning burned off, residual energy vented safely. At some point: The inflow exceeded processing capacity Or the furnace mechanisms failed Or Hell was damaged during a metaphysical war Doesn’t matter which. The result is the same. Hell stopped processing souls and started storing them. Consequences? Pressure builds, exhaust backs up, heat leaks. That leak is magic. Its like the world is on top of a radioactive dump, but instead of cancer, the people get EROSION.

item.title: THE GREAT HEARTH
item.description: A movement striving to unite all of humanity in one giant Hearth, they believe that will stabilise everyone and bring about the harmony observed among the more stable Hearths. Their methods are faulty, unorthodox, and oftentimes forceful, which turned the public opinion against them after some disastrous attempts at joining 20 people on a city square that no longer exists...Individual identity is the problem. They talk about equilibrium and "global balance". Forced soul-binding rituals Artifact-driven mass merges City-scale bonding attempts Kidnapping Embers first (they’re “keystones”) Because every failure: Produces survivors who swear it almost worked. Creates permanent hot zones they interpret as “holy ground”. Generates Cinders they call Heralds.

item.title: CASE STUDY
item.description: Population: ~12,000 Status: Regional hub, not a capital Age: Old enough to be layered with mistakes The Look & Tech Level (Dieselgrim, Not Fancy) Power & Light Central oil-fired generator complex (“The Heartstack”) Network of steam pipes and fuel lines Lantern pylons every 30–50 meters Street lights burn 24/7 Backup braziers in every district Blackouts are not tolerated. Executions have happened over “maintenance failures.” Architecture Stone lower floors (fire resistance) Iron-framed upper levels Narrow streets to reduce Mist pooling Wide, well-lit plazas as thermal relief zones Everything smells faintly of: Oil Ash Hot metal No wood unless treated. The Grey Mist Situation Brightfall exists in a Mist basin. Inside the City Light keeps the Mist thin but never gone Morning fog is not normal fog Citizens wear scarves or filter masks as habit Children are taught: “If you can’t see the lantern across the street, you go home.” Outside the Walls Mist thickens within minutes Sound dampens Fire behaves erratically Road markers are lit constantly Travelers arrive shaken, coughing, or wrong. Food & Survival (This Is Where Grimdark Gets Mundane) Food Sources Root vegetables (grown underground with heat lamps) Fungal farms (Mist-tolerant strains) River fish (considered risky but protein-rich) Preserved meats (smoked heavily) Fresh fruit is: Rare Expensive Considered medicinal Food Safety Cooking isn’t just culinary. It’s sterilization: Heat kills Mist residue Raw food is taboo Fireless meals are distrusted Starvation kills fewer people than cold contamination. Living Conditions (Class Is Measured in Degrees) Upper Districts Close to Heartstack Bright, dry, noisy Lower Mist exposure Expensive rents Ember housing heavily regulated These people live longer. Lower Districts Near walls or old burn zones Lantern gaps Thicker Mist Higher collapse rates Slums aren’t lawless. They’re thermally neglected. Embers, Hearths & City Infrastructure Hearth Registry Every Hearth in Brightfall is: Registered Assigned inspection dates Required to attend cooling rites Unregistered Hearths are illegal. Not immoral. Illegal. Release Infrastructure Three public release halls One military-only vent tower Countless illegal micro-vents in slums Public release days are tense but routine. Think: Long lines Silent prayers Fire crews on standby Internal Security (Order Is Non-Negotiable) City Watch Heavily equipped with: Fire-resistant gear Cooling rods Mist dispersal grenades They are trained less to fight people and more to contain events. Collapse Response Units Separate from the Watch. Rapid deployment Authorized to kill without trial Tasked with isolating Hollow events Everyone hates them. Everyone wants them nearby. Religion & Public Space Dominant Faith: The Vigil of Ash Core beliefs: Light is duty Fire is burden Collapse is failure of care, not sin Temples double as: Lantern hubs Cooling shelters Hearth bonding sites Priests carry thermometers and ledgers. Government & Politics Governance Model Council of Fuel, Light, and Watch No single ruler All decisions filtered through risk impact Democracy exists in theory. In practice: The lanterns decide. Taxes Paid in: Coin Fuel Mandatory service rotations Miss fuel tax and your district dims. People notice. External Relations Trade Fuel imports (critical) Export: treated metals, vented slag, trained personnel Brightfall sells: people who know how to survive fire. Diplomacy Neighbors tolerate Brightfall because: It maintains roads It clears Mist paths It handles Cinders that wander too close In exchange: Tribute fuel Political leverage The Cult Problem (Because Of Course) The Great Hearth Movement has cells in Brightfall. Current Status Officially banned Quietly persistent Recruiting among: Embers Slum dwellers Collapse survivors They believe Brightfall is: “A model city—ready to be unified.” The city council is aware. They are terrified. Daily Life (The Human Bit) People: Walk briskly Avoid emotional outbursts Keep lantern oil on hand Don’t gather in darkness Children play games about: Keeping fires balanced “Don’t burn your friends” Love exists. It’s just… careful. Final Snapshot Brightfall isn’t heroic. It isn’t evil. It’s working. Which, in this world, is the closest thing to hope. Currency & Economy: Heat Is the Real Backing Forget gold standards. Gold doesn’t vent. What Money Actually Represents Currency is backed by: Light Fuel Containment capacity The more you can keep places lit and people cooled, the more stable your economy. Common forms: Fuel chits (oil, tallow, treated coal) Lantern credits Release vouchers (dangerously valuable) Heat-ration tokens for Embers Counterfeiting isn’t about metal. It’s about lying about safety. Wealth Is Distance From Heat Rich people: Live in well-lit districts Far from release sites Surrounded by stone, water, and warding Poor people: Live in the Mist Near old burn zones Serve as accidental heat sinks Class isn’t abstract. It’s thermal. Values & Philosophy: Restraint Over Virtue Heroism gets you killed. Core Cultural Virtues Self-control Cowardice (rebranded as prudence) Predictability Collective risk minimization People don’t ask: “Is it right?” They ask: “Will this raise the temperature?” Common Saying “Don’t be a spark.” That’s an insult. Religion: Containment, Not Salvation Most religions gave up on after. Now they deal with before. Mainstream Doctrine The soul is a liability Life is a containment exercise Death is a failure to manage residue Priests: Maintain lanterns Bless release chambers Conduct cooling rites Keep records of dangerous lineages Confession isn’t for forgiveness. It’s for risk assessment. Heresies (Popular Ones) Hell can be repaired Collapse is transcendence Fire purifies (these people don’t last) Government: Risk Management With Bad Optics States don’t rule. They mitigate. Core State Functions Light infrastructure Ember registry Hearth licensing Collapse response units Cinder extermination Elections, crowns, councils — flavor. The real power is whoever controls: Fuel Release sites Bonding rites Martial Law Is Permanent Not dramatic. Routine. Nobody riots over curfews. They riot over lantern outages.

item.title: THE EARTHBOUND
item.description: These zealots believe in extremist pacifism, claiming that every life spared and death delayed brings HELL closer to catching up, and ridding the world of magic and GREY MIST.

item.title: ASH
item.description: Cost: 2 Energy. EXHAUST when played. RETAIN

item.title: CURSES
item.description: Adding a CURSES Keyword to a card makes it add a random STATUScard into the shared deck when played, But, it reduces the card's overall cost by 2 ENERGY (effectively -1 reduction as CURSESare still technically keywords and so they increase a card's cost by 1).

item.title: EROSION
item.description: Cost: 1 Energy. When drawn, take 1 DMG. DRAW 1 card when played.

item.title: RETAIN
item.description: Card stays in hand if unplayed.

item.title: DELAY
item.description: Card takes effect at the start of next turn.

item.title: Shrieker
item.description: GUARD (4)STRIKE (6)STRIKE (14)HP = 9

item.title: Husk
item.description: STRIKE (6)GUARD (6)STRIKE (8)GUARD (12)HP = 7

item.title: EROSION
item.description: Cost: 1 Energy. When drawn, take 1 DMG. DRAW 1 card when played.

item.title: HOLLOW
item.description: Cost: 1 Energy. No effect.

item.title: ASH
item.description: Cost: 2 Energy. Exhaust when played. RETAIN

item.title: EXHAUST
item.description: Cards with this keyword are removed from the deck after being played.

item.title: MIST
item.description: When drawn, shuffle a MIST into the Draw Pile. RETAIN

item.title: OATH_OF_THE_CRUCIBLE
item.description:
Your Prism scores are as follows: 2 Might
1 Guile
0 Insight You may allocate a single point to a chosen Prism. Your Starting deck consists of: Either;
6x Strike
4x Guard Or;
6x Guard
4x Strike

item.title: WARD
item.description: Negates the next negative STATUS or Keyword applied by an enemy. Stacks.

item.title: RETAIN
item.description: Card stays in hand if unplayed.

item.title: IGNITE
item.description: Deal Value DMG at the end of the turn, then decrease stacks by 1. Scales with INSIGHT

item.title: ECHO
item.description: Retrigger previous card (with a matching Primary) with all values set to 1.

item.title: DRAW
item.description: Draw X cards.

item.title: DISCARD
item.description: Discard X cards.

item.title: DELAY
item.description: Card takes effect at the start of next turn.

item.title: BLEED
item.description: Deal Value DMG again at the end of enemy's turn, once. Scales with GUILE

item.title: STRIKE
item.description: Deal Value DMG. Scales with MIGHT

item.title: FOCUS
item.description: Adds Value to the numeric effect of the next card played. Scales with INSIGHT

item.title: COUNTER
item.description: Grants Value stacks. Deals Value DMG back when hit; halve stacks (round up) after each trigger. Scales with GUILE
